incidents.victim: US critical infrastructure (water, wastewater, energy, municipal governments)
incidents.vector: Iranian-affiliated actors exploited internet-exposed Rockwell Automation and Allen-Bradley programmable logic controllers (PLCs) and related SCADA/HMI devices; Unit 42 links the activity to the Cyber Av3ngers cluster and observed ~5,600 exposed devices globally.
incidents.impact: Joint FBI/DoD/NSA advisory confirms operational disruption and financial losses at multiple US entities since March 2026, including a water plant in Minot, ND and a county government in Indiana; tied to escalating cyber retaliation following US/Israel strikes on Iran.

incidents.victim: TeamPCP supply-chain targets (LiteLLM, Checkmarx, Trivy, Telnyx PyPI, VS Code and Docker Hub ecosystems)
incidents.vector: TeamPCP used a stolen maintainer credential to push credential-harvesting payloads into trusted packages (LiteLLM ~97M monthly downloads, Checkmarx GitHub Actions) and chained across five software ecosystems in five days; earlier compromised Trivy/Aqua Security and ran self-propagating worm against exposed Docker APIs, Kubernetes, Redis.
incidents.impact: Tens of thousands of cloud credentials, API keys, and secrets exfiltrated; group claims 300GB stolen from extortion victims and is monetizing the data via ransomware and dark-web sales; SANS ISC and Insikt Group tracking ongoing downstream exploitation.

incidents.victim: Microsoft customers (SharePoint Server, Windows Defender)
incidents.vector: Patch Tuesday addressed 165–167 vulnerabilities, including actively exploited CVE-2026-32201 (SharePoint Server spoofing) and publicly disclosed CVE-2026-33825 (BlueHammer, Windows Defender local privilege escalation) with PoC exploit code released by researcher "Chaotic Eclipse".
incidents.impact: CISA added CVE-2026-32201 to the KEV catalog; exploitation risk elevated given the size of the patch batch and public exploit code for BlueHammer; Adobe separately patched CVE-2026-34621 in Acrobat/Reader exploited in the wild since at least late November 2025.

incidents.victim: Cloud workloads on AWS, Google Cloud, Azure, Alibaba
incidents.vector: A stripped, statically-linked ELF backdoor attributed to APT41 probes cloud instance metadata services (including 169.254.169.254) to harvest temporary credentials, uses SMTP port 25 as a covert C2 channel invisible to Shodan/Censys scanners, and hides behind three typosquatted Alibaba/Qianxin lookalike domains.
incidents.impact: Zero-detection backdoor with no VirusTotal hits enables cross-cloud credential theft and persistent access across Linux cloud workloads; represents six years of APT41 investment in cloud-native tooling, threatening multi-tenant enterprise environments.

incidents.victim: OpenAI (macOS code-signing certificate) and Axios npm downstream users
incidents.vector: North Korean actors (UNC1069) socially engineered the Axios npm library's lead maintainer, took over his npm and GitHub accounts, and pushed two malicious Axios versions live for ~3 hours before removal; malware flowed into dependent packages with >100M weekly downloads.
incidents.impact: OpenAI revoking and rotating its macOS code-signing certificate after a GitHub workflow executed the compromised Axios; older OpenAI macOS apps will stop working after May 8; Google TIG warned of broad ripple effects across the JavaScript ecosystem.

incidents.victim: Dutch hospitals (via ChipSoft / HiX EHR platform)
incidents.vector: Ransomware attack on April 7 against Dutch healthcare software vendor ChipSoft, whose HiX platform serves ~70% of Dutch hospitals; unauthorized access to ChipSoft systems forced a precautionary shutdown of Zorgportaal, HiX Mobile, and Zorgplatform.
incidents.impact: 11 hospitals (including Sint Jans Gasthuis Weert, Laurentius Roermond, VieCuri Venlo, Flevo Almere) severed VPN links to ChipSoft; LUMC postponed a new EHR rollout; ChipSoft cannot rule out patient-data exposure; Z-CERT says no critical care halted but logistical disruption continues.

incidents.victim: Canadian employees of organizations using Microsoft 365 and Workday
incidents.vector: Microsoft DART tracked Storm-2755 using SEO poisoning and malvertising (domain bluegraintours[.]com) to redirect victims to fake Microsoft 365 login pages, then an adversary-in-the-middle proxy via Axios 1.7.9 to steal session tokens and bypass MFA, followed by Workday payroll redirection.
incidents.impact: At least one confirmed victim had their payroll direct-deposit diverted to attacker-controlled bank accounts; inbox rules hid the attack from victims; campaign targeted Canadian users broadly via geographic filtering rather than a specific industry.

incidents.victim: Northern Ireland schools (Education Authority C2K network)
incidents.vector: Cyberattack against the centrally managed C2K IT platform (operated with Capita) supporting Northern Ireland schools; EA shut down access to contain the incident and is resetting passwords in phased restoration.
incidents.impact: Disruption for roughly 300,000 pupils and 20,000 teachers across Northern Ireland; priority restoration for exam-sitting students; EA says no evidence yet of data exfiltration but investigation continuing.

incidents.victim: Courtney Williams / US Army Special Military Unit classified information
incidents.vector: Former Army employee with TS/SCI clearance allegedly communicated for years with a journalist (180+ messages, 10+ hours of calls) and disclosed classified national defense information used in a book and article; also posted on her social media.
incidents.impact: Indicted under 18 USC 793(d); DOJ/FBI say disclosures put US warfighters, allies, and intelligence tradecraft of the Special Military Unit at risk; case underscores insider-risk exposure for classified units.

incidents.victim: Minnesota — Winona County government
incidents.vector: Cyberattack disrupted Winona County IT networks and public services; Governor Walz activated the Minnesota National Guard's cyber units to assist recovery. Separate from the county's late-January ransomware incident.
incidents.impact: Degraded county government services; National Guard deployed to assist containment; second significant cyber incident at Winona County within three months.

incidents.victim: Los Angeles City Attorney's Office (incl. LAPD records)
incidents.vector: Attackers breached the LA City Attorney's Office and exfiltrated 7.7 TB of sensitive data before publishing it online.
incidents.impact: Leaked trove includes LAPD officer personal information, witness names, medical records, and internal-affairs investigation files — a significant law-enforcement data exposure affecting officer safety and active cases.

incidents.victim: ~18,000 SOHO routers (TP-Link, MikroTik) in 120+ countries; 200+ organizations impacted
incidents.vector: GRU actors exploited known TP-Link/MikroTik vulnerabilities to steal credentials and modify DNS settings on thousands of end-of-life SOHO routers, redirecting traffic to attacker DNS resolvers to stage adversary-in-the-middle attacks against Outlook Web Access and harvest Microsoft OAuth tokens, credentials, emails.
incidents.impact: FBI/DOJ Operation Masquerade court-authorized remediation of US routers across 23+ states; Microsoft and Lumen confirm victims in government, IT, telecom, energy sectors and an unnamed European national identity platform; campaign activity has ceased but compromise may persist on unremediated devices.

incidents.victim: Pinterest, Chime Financial
incidents.vector: Pro-Iranian hacktivist group launched distributed-denial-of-service attacks against Pinterest and fintech Chime Financial, taking Pinterest offline for several hours.
incidents.impact: Temporary service outages at two US consumer platforms; low-sophistication disruption consistent with Unit 42's assessment of ongoing Iran-aligned hacktivist activity during the US-Iran conflict.

incidents.victim: FortiClient EMS customers globally (~2,000 exposed instances)
incidents.vector: Attackers began exploiting CVE-2026-35616, an unauthenticated CVSS 9.8 flaw in Fortinet FortiClient EMS, on March 31; Fortinet released an emergency hotfix April 4 but the full patch is pending.
incidents.impact: CISA added the bug to the KEV catalog with a Thursday mitigation deadline; Singapore CSA issued parallel advisory; watchTowr honeypots observed exploitation ramping up, with ShadowServer identifying nearly 2,000 internet-exposed FortiClient EMS instances.

incidents.victim: University of Mississippi Medical Center, Passaic County NJ, healthcare/education/finance orgs in US/UK/AU
incidents.vector: Storm-1175 weaponizes newly disclosed N-day vulnerabilities (and in some cases zero-days a week before disclosure, including CVE-2026-23760 SmarterMail and CVE-2025-10035 GoAnywhere MFT) in web-facing systems; moves from initial access to Medusa ransomware deployment in as little as 24 hours.
incidents.impact: Recent victims include UMMC (fully reopened March 2 with FBI/DHS help), New Jersey's Passaic County, and organizations in healthcare, education, professional services, and finance; Microsoft warns of escalating "perimeter-first" ransomware trend.

incidents.victim: Enterprise networks running Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center
incidents.vector: Interlock ransomware group exploited CVE-2026-20131, a critical unauthenticated Java deserialization flaw in Cisco Secure FMC, as a zero-day starting January 26, 2026, deploying a malicious ELF binary from 37.27.244[.]222 and custom Java/JavaScript RATs plus ConnectWise ScreenConnect for persistence.
incidents.impact: Amazon Threat Intelligence linked the campaign to enterprise network compromises preceding Cisco's March 4 public disclosure; public PoC exploit on GitHub (sak110) increases broader exploitation risk.

incidents.victim: Jones Day (US law firm)
incidents.vector: Successful phishing attack allowed hackers to access Jones Day client files; stolen customer materials were subsequently posted online by the attackers.
incidents.impact: One of the largest US corporate law firms (2,400+ lawyers, including former representation of President Trump's 2016 and 2020 campaigns) confirmed client data exposure; raises downstream client confidentiality and legal-privilege concerns.

incidents.victim: Bitcoin Depot (crypto ATM operator)
incidents.vector: Attackers breached Bitcoin Depot's internal crypto-wallets in March 2026 and drained funds; disclosed via SEC 8-K filing on April 6.
incidents.impact: $3.6 million in cryptocurrency stolen from the operator of 25,000+ Bitcoin ATMs worldwide; incident underscores persistent targeting of crypto-infrastructure operators.

incidents.victim: Drift (Solana-based DeFi platform)
incidents.vector: DPRK actors (Citrine Sleet / UNC4736 / AppleJeus) posed as a legitimate trading firm, met Drift employees at conferences, then compromised their devices via a malicious Apple TestFlight app and poisoned VS Code extensions to access multi-sig wallet keys.
incidents.impact: $280 million stolen from Drift's treasury in a March 2026 heist after months of DPRK social engineering; another major state-sponsored crypto theft funding North Korea's weapons programs.

incidents.victim: Rogers, Freedom Mobile, Fido (Canadian telcos)
incidents.vector: Telus Digital (a Canadian business-process outsourcing provider) was breached, leading to customer-data exposure at multiple downstream telcos that relied on Telus for support services.
incidents.impact: Rogers and Quebecor-owned Freedom Mobile and Fido notified subscribers of data breaches traced to the Telus Digital compromise; scope of affected records not publicly quantified.

incidents.victim: Zephyr Energy (UK; US subsidiary)
incidents.vector: Business email compromise: attackers tricked staff at Zephyr Energy's US subsidiary into redirecting a contractor payment to an attacker-controlled account.
incidents.impact: €700,000 (~$760K) stolen from a UK-listed oil & gas firm; classic BEC loss underscores continued effectiveness of payment-redirect fraud against mid-cap energy firms.

incidents.victim: China National Supercomputing Center (NSCC)
incidents.vector: Attacker group "FlamingChina" (first observed February 2026) exfiltrated petabytes of data from China's National Supercomputing Center and published it online in March 2026; authenticity took weeks to confirm due to the dataset's scale.
incidents.impact: Some leaked files marked "secret" contain apparent military and government data; significant intelligence loss for the Chinese government — a rare public hack-and-leak against Beijing's high-performance computing infrastructure.
